sentences.text: About February of '48.
We went from New York.
We caught a train across-- we stopped over in Chicago and then finished up in San Francisco, and then flew from San Francisco to Sydney.
Pan Am Clipper.
Took three days.
It was very depressing, if truth be told.

Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as far as I was concerned, I went from a normal household, where I lived in a small country town in the United States, with-- with my aunt and uncle and his three daughters.
And they had boyfriends and cars.
And they used to take me everywhere, because my aunt insisted on it.
So they were teenagers or early in their 20s, and they didn't want me.
But I insisted that I had to be taken everywhere-- to the movies, to see Humphrey Bogart movies and all that.
And it was really great.
And all of a sudden, I was back-- well, not back.
But I was in this city, living in a little hotel called the Kirketon Hotel, at the Cross, which was a very dingy place and not knowing anybody.
And after a week, we were thrown out, because it was the Easter Show, and they had-- the hotel was booked out.
So that my parents couldn't understand why, you know?
Having lived in Paris, which, which is a bit more sophisticated, why, after just one week, we are thrown out when we are willing to pay for staying there?
But the reason was there was few in the way of-- very few rooms there.
And they were all taken up by the Easter Show.
So then we moved into a boarding house in Bellevue Hill here, not very far from here, which is a one-room in the front, where I used to sleep with my parents.
They had one bed and I had a small bed.
And the-- and the guy used to come once, once a week, with a big ice block that used to be put into this refrigerator.
Now this was-- I mean, I don't want to complain.
But this was, again, a retrograde step, when they had-- they even had television in America.
So there was no television, there was no refrigeration, there was no bedroom for me.
There was no, no nothing.
And no friends, you know.
And my parents weren't terribly happy, either.
Because my mother was separated from her one surviving sister that, that she had.
She'd lost all her family.
And she only ever saw her once more in all those years.
Because she didn't-- we couldn't afford to travel back anymore.
And by the time I could afford to send her, she was already a very old woman.
I went to Double Bay Primary School.
I should have gone to already into high school.
But again, I hadn't had very much in the way of schooling.
So they-- they thought that I wasn't ready to go into high school.
So they put me into Double Bay Primary School.
So I was always a year older than all the other children, which was a bit of a problem, I thought.
And then when I-- the year finished, I only got into a three-year high school.
In those days, they used to have these intermediate high schools, where you only could go to the three years version.
You couldn't do your leaving certificate, or the HSC, which it's called now.
And as a result of that, you could only get a job as a tradesman, as a-- whatever.
And you couldn't study, you couldn't go to university after that.
So I-- this was really very bad.
Because I really was very good at languages, obviously, because I'd spoken all those languages before.
I'd spoken French, went to a French school, Hungarian, Romanian, Polish, you know.
So this was my forte.
And I had to go-- so I said to my father that I don't really want to go to this high school.
So he said, who is the Minister for Education?
And it was Mr.
Heffron.
So he said, get me an appointment with the Minister for Education.
[LAUGHS]
And the minister-- the office was in, in Bligh Street, in the city, those old buildings which are still there.
And there was this huge office there.
And this man was sitting behind a desk.
And we came in, and he said, yes?
And I said, my father would like me to go to a proper high school.
And [LAUGHS] Mr.
Heffron was so flabbergasted that this little kid came and spoke to him directly, that he put me into Randwick Boys' High School.
Otherwise I think my career would have been curtailed somewhat.
So I think that's a reflection of what sort of man my father was, that he did that sort of thing.
Because you know, a little migrant child, going to see the Minister for Education, not-- not someone underneath.
I do not even know how I managed to get an appointment, because my father didn't speak English enough.
But we went straight to the top.
I remember Mr.
Heffron very well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I went to Randwick Boys' High.
There were a lot of migrant kids there.
This was better by now, because there were a lot of-- Poles-- well, mostly Hungarians, actually-- where I made some lifelong friends whom I still have.
A lot of them-- a lot of us did medicine from there.
It was made up of a very large number, because it was in the eastern suburbs.
And because they were migrants, they couldn't get into Sydney High School, which was sort of-- you were graded according to your standard of education.
And the top people used to go to Sydney High.
And the next echelon was-- was Randwick.
So we were all, most of us, in Randwick, and I went there.
Yes, I did.
I had a bar mitzvah in-- I was 13 in-- 19-- 1959, isn't it?
From '36, is that right?
Yeah, I had it at the-- the old Central Synagogue, which no longer exists.
It's now that expressway down there.
And I had all my big do, there were about 10 people [LAUGHS] that came, because we didn't have many friends yet.
And I went to Hebrew classes with the rabbi.
And he actually had a large influence on me, as far as religious teaching was concerned and dietary laws.
He was a very good teacher.
And I became more kosher than my parents ever were.
My parents were a bit more cynical about that, because, I suppose, what happened to-- to them and their families and so on.
But I, to this day have remained relatively kosher, I suppose, in the sense that I don't eat any pork or shellfish or any of those things that everybody thinks is so fantastic.
Apart from all those years ago in Hungary, I no longer eat it.
I had not a very happy teenage years.
We used to-- after this, we graduated from this one room to a small flat in Bondi Junction.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I was the only child, and my parents went to work.
And after school, I used to-- my mother worked for [INAUDIBLE], which was a manufacturing company, as a dispatcher.
And I was afraid to stay in the flat by myself.
So after school, I used to go by tram to the city and wait for her to come out of the factory at 5:30, whatever it was, and come home with her.
I couldn't stay home.
I did this for years, which is most unusual for, for, for someone like that.
I had this sort of bond with my mother.
And I couldn't be separated, apart from the time I went to school.
The rest of the time, I couldn't be left alone.
I, I had a big problem with that.
And I think that affected me, oh, I suppose a lot, in lots of ways-- in relationships and so on.
But this fear of being alone, I'm not sure.
That must have stemmed from my upbringing, I suppose.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it affected me in lots of other ways.
Because this bond that was a very-- I mean, I suppose it was a very good bond, between mother and son.
But it became unhealthy in later life.
Because my mother then became the-- as she became older, she became more dependent on me.
Whereas I broke loose from this-- from these shackles, I suppose, she withdrew into it as she got older.
And therefore, I had to look after her.
And it was very difficult when I went to England to study for my postgraduate course in surgery.
I felt very bad about leaving my mother.
My father was OK, but my mother was so dependent on me, being the only child.
And I think it did affect me as far, as far as my relationships with other people.
And I think it was an unhealthy relationship, by that stage, with-- with one's parents.
I think-- I have three children of my own.
And I've tried not to emulate that sort of mistake.
I think that they should be independent.
Well, I had a, a sort of relatively smooth transition in, in high school.
I did reasonably well and went on to medicine.
I never failed medicine.
I didn't fail any of, of my years.
I passed every year.
But I had-- I think it affected my career in the long term.
Whatever success I've had, I think I could have been more successful.
The reason why wasn't is because of this-- the fact that I couldn't break out of this, this stranglehold, really, that I had with, with my mother.
I, I wasn't confident enough in myself to, to-- I think I gained my confidence much later.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I was confident enough to, to become a surgeon.
To be a surgeon, you have to be very, very sure of yourself.
You have to be very confident of yourself, right?
Now, it's very difficult for someone like me to do that.
And I think I could have become a bigger success, if I hadn't had this-- this sort of upbringing, I think.
Not that I regret it, you know.
I mean, it's just part of the thing.
But I think I could have done better.
I mean, I'm sure all of us think that.

But I-- I personally think that I could have done much, much better, if only I would have had the strength of my convictions and believed in myself, that I was confident enough and, and, and that I was able to do things, which I didn't.
Which, obviously, I was able to, but I didn't have the confidence to pursue it.
So I had to curtail a lot of my activities.
I came back earlier than I should have.
I should have had more experience overseas.
I should have perhaps gone into-- more into academia, more into hospital work.
There were lots of things
Yes.
As a surgeon, yeah.
I married late.
I married when I was 32.
The reason for that was because, although my friends were married-- in those days they married very early-- I was one of the last ones to get married of my peer group.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My parents were not happily married, for various reasons.
And I think I didn't want to make the same mistake.
Plus, I didn't have anyone to really talk over things with, you see.
My-- my mother, god bless her heart, was very set in her ways, and you can understand why.
And there was no one.
I missed the fact that I didn't have any grandparents, or any siblings, or any, any close family that I could actually come to, you know, when I had my problems, you see.
When I first, first fell in love with the first woman I ever fell in love with, I wanted to marry.
You know, my parents said, you're too young, you can't do it.
And I wanted to talk over this with someone that I could, you see?
And I couldn't find anybody to do this.
So I listened to my parents.
And of course-- and then I waited, and time went by.
And I was 32 before I finally got married.
And I married someone who I thought I was in love with.
I probably was.
And she was Jewish, and she was from the same background.
Her parents [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] are Polish or were Polish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was much younger than I was.
She was 12 years younger than I was.
But she was 20 and I was 32.
But everything seemed to be OK.
And for a while it did.
But it didn't work out.
We divorced after 12 years of marriage.
Paula.
I had three, three daughters.
Heidi, Tanya, and Claudia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we split up when they were very small.
They were five, seven, and nine.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So I found myself back to where I started from, in the sense of trying to build up a, a family, a happy family, which I had lacked for various reasons over the years.
It seemed to have eluded me.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And just when-- when I was, how shall I put it, at the peak of my career, I was in my mid-40s, I was getting somewhere, I was getting more confident, I had a stable home, I had three lovely daughters.
I had the-- you know, obviously successful enough to afford the things I wanted, it all caved in on me again.
And virtually I came back to almost square one, you know?
And I had to change a lot of things.
I'm not approportion-- I'm not proportioning blame on her or on me, or whatever.

I know a lot of people are judgmental about what happened, because they feel that if a woman wants out of a marriage with three children, especially three daughters, there is something wrong with her, you see?
And so I suppose if you look-- if you were to ask some of my friends, they would say that it was her fault, and everything-- and I was the saint.
Be that as it may, I don't-- I don't believe that.
I think I was a good husband.
Like I was a good child, I was a good husband.
But she married for, for the wrong reasons.
And she was a strong enough personality to decide that she really didn't want this sort of thing.
She really-- this is not the sort of life she wanted.
And I don't think she was ready to take on the responsibilities of marriage and three children, the whole thing.
She wanted a different life.
She wanted to be an actress, and this.
And then I think-- and in lots of ways I think that-- well, if people watch this tape-- it's the truth, anyway, what difference does it make?
I think she was pushed by her-- by her mother.
Which is not unusual for, you know, for a Jewish mother to want her daughter to marry a Jewish doctor.
I mean, it's epitome of, of success, isn't it?
And when you're 20-- and I think back now, I see my own children.
I mean, at the age of 20, not my daughters would not know who to marry.
And if they came along with a guy who was Jewish, who was 32, was a doctor, I'd throw up my hands in horror and say, this is disgusting, you know?
You raised your daughters?
Yes.
Well, they stayed with me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for lots-- for-- well, for reasons which, which I thought was best.
We have a good relationship with their mother to this day.
And she even felt it would, would be better if there was less disruption to their childhood, if she stayed in the house.
They went to Moriah College up the street.
And she had rights to come in anytime she wanted.
And we had a very, very easy relationship and even to this day.
I'm not saying there isn't any resentment, I'm sure there is.
[LAUGHS] But as far as broken marriages is-- is concerned, it's, it's, it's pretty good.
And I married, yes, nearly eight years ago, yeah.
Gee, I'd better-- I'd better get this right, don't I?
[LAUGHS] Now you're asking me things which happened only a short time ago.
It was the 11th of December, eight years ago.
Danielle.
Grynberg.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She-- she was married before.
She married actually-- her first husband was not Jewish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- although she is Jewish.
She has a stepson-- she has-- I have a stepson, she has a son, who also-- well, nobody's living in the house now.
But they all lived-- we all lived together here.
Trent.
Well, I tried to-- to give them the sort of childhood that I didn't have, obviously.
But I didn't succeed, in the sense that I think they were traumatized in a different way.
Because I think that, it doesn't matter whether it's my marriage you're talking about or any marriage in which there is a divorce, it affects, it affects the children.
I think that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think that they've come through it fairly well.
Or very well, maybe.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But things could have been better.
No.
The only thing I dream of occasionally is, is my wife leaving me.
I think that's the sense of insecurity.
But that's becoming less and less.
[LAUGHS]
On my attitude, yes, not on my health, I don't think.
I mean, I've got high blood pressure and high cholesterol level and whatever.
But I mean, I don't think that I can blame that on my, my childhood experiences.
But I'm sure my, my attitude to certain things, yeah.
That's a very difficult question.
Well, I think that when we pass on, it probably shouldn't be lost.
And I think what, what you're doing now is, is, is important.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think there's lots of things to be learned from it.
It's, it's, it's hard to imagine how, how it could have happened, and, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and how the world could have allowed it to happen.
But what's happening now in other parts of the world, it makes you understand how difficult it is to control it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think that democracy is, is an incredible thing.
We may grumble about the fact that John Howard isn't charismatic, but give me John Howard anytime.
I'd rather have him than Adolf Hitler.
So although they may not do anything and grumble and complain and so on, it is still the best form of democracy that you can have.
No, I've tried-- I've tried to sort of put it behind me, in a sense, right?
I've tried to fight against my inadequacies, which I, which I understand.
I'm well aware of, of my problems.
I always have been, and I've fought them along the way.
And to the best of my ability, I've managed to-- to overcome them, I think, you know.
And I'm happy with my lot.
I'm happily married now.
My wife is a fantastic woman.
And I'm not only saying this because she's going to watch this, but because she is.
She's also helped me to raise my children.
Because when we were married, they were still very young.
It wasn't easy for her to come into a situation like she came in, with three children from, from somebody else.
And she's done a fantastic job.
And I'm-- I, I don't think I could have done it without her.
I mean, I tried for a few years on my own.
But I think they wouldn't have turned out the way they have, if she hadn't helped me.
She's a very intelligent and able woman who understands [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the problems.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's one thing to, to tell a child off if you're the parent, another to tell it if you're the stepparent.
They don't tolerate that very well.
I know it's not right, but that's the way human nature is.
But I think we're over the worst of it.
I think that the, the children are hopefully now on their way that they can fend for themselves.
And I can perhaps now relax.
If there are, I've some-- I think I've suppressed quite a bit.
I feel there's something still in my mind which, as we've been talking this afternoon, it hasn't come out.
And I'm not sure why I've suppressed it.

Maybe it's more traumatic than I imagine, or maybe it has it had an influence on my performance or thinking, et cetera, that, that, I've suppressed it to such an extent that I can't-- I can't bring it out of the, the deep recesses of my mind.
Maybe under hypnotherapy I could.
But maybe I don't want to do that.
Maybe it's not necessary.
I think it's been traumatic enough.
And I didn't realize that just retelling the story that I would be so emotional [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to me, now it's been such a long time.
[SIGHS] I'm glad I'm Jewish, in spite of everything.
I think-- and I've tried to instill this in my children.
And I put them through a Jewish school.

Because I think that there are now such forces outside, in which-- which influence us to such an extent that there is enough out there-- how shall I put it-- that will take away from our Jewishness, you see?
And if you don't give them this sort of background to start off with, there's no point blaming them, if things go against what you think is right.
Because you haven't instilled them into it.
If they do things which you don't think is right, at least you've given that start in life.
And they can make up their own mind.
The most important thing is, is happiness, of course.
But I think it's good to have a Jewish upbringing.
My first wife was against-- she's more, I'll say, liberal.
She wanted them to have a more rounded education and meet people of, of other races and other religions.
Because it tends to be a little bit homophobic being in a Jewish school.
But I don't think that this has happened.
Because my children now who are out there either in the workforce or studying at-- are meeting people of other races and other creeds and other religions and so on.
And they are doing very well.
OK.
Thank you.
